planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; seeds germinate in late winter to early spring.

directions: Bexar Clover is a small native annual of dry, rocky, calcareous, or sandy open ground in Texas and adjacent south-central states, blooming from March through May with compact, globe-shaped cream-white to pale pink flower heads. Sow seeds directly in fall into lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; no soil amendment is needed, and fertility encourages weeds at the expense of this clover. An ecologically valuable species that supports specialist native bee communities in the south-central US, it self-sows freely and naturalizes reliably in open, disturbed ground with minimal competition.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scatter seeds directly in fall into thin, dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; germination occurs naturally in late winter.

directions: Carolina Clover is a delicate, small native annual of dry, open, thin-soiled, and disturbed ground throughout the southeastern US, producing characteristic trifoliate leaves and tiny white to pale pink flower heads in spring — one of the few truly native Trifolium species in the region. Sow directly in fall into thin, dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; it thrives on neglect and competes poorly in rich, heavily mulched beds. An important early spring nectar source for bumble bees, mason bees, and small native bee species, it self-sows prolifically and works well naturalized in thin-soiled areas, native meadow plots, or low-maintenance lawn areas as an ecologically superior alternative to non-native clovers.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States, Weakley et al.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in early spring.

directions: Buffalo Clover is a native annual or biennial clover of dry to moderately moist, open, disturbed ground throughout the eastern and central US, historically associated with bison grazing lands and open forest clearings. It produces showy bicolored flower heads — creamy-white banner petals contrasting with pink to red wing petals — making it among the most ornamental of the native US clovers, blooming in spring and early summer. Direct sow in fall into average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; it tolerates moderate soil fertility and self-sows in open, disturbed ground, providing nectar for bumblebees and small native bees.

planting.note: Transplant nursery-grown plugs in spring into moderately disturbed, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; cold stratification improves germination if starting from seed.

directions: Running Buffalo Clover is a federally endangered native perennial once widespread across open, disturbed, grassy habitats — woodland edges, forest openings, and bison-grazed clearings — throughout the eastern and central US, now restricted to a small number of conservation sites in West Virginia, Ohio, Indiana, Kentucky, and Missouri. It spreads by stolons in a strawberry-like fashion, forming low mats of trifoliate leaves dotted with small cream-white clover flower heads; its decline is directly tied to the loss of frequent, moderate disturbance from bison grazing. Obtain plants from nurseries specializing in federally listed native species and transplant into moderately disturbed, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; periodic light mowing or disturbance mimics the historic grazing regime that this species depends on.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing and direct sow in fall into dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; the deep taproot establishes during the first growing season and the plant blooms fully in subsequent years.

directions: Slimflower Scurfpea is a deep-rooted native perennial legume of dry, open prairies, sandy plains, and disturbed ground throughout the Great Plains and south-central US, producing wiry, much-branched stems with delicate pinnate leaves covered in tiny resinous glands and loose clusters of blue-violet, pea-like flowers from May through July. Scarify seeds mechanically or with sandpaper before sowing to improve germination rates, then direct sow in fall into dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun — avoid fertile or amended soil, which favors weedy competition over this tough prairie native. The extensive taproot makes established plants extremely drought-tolerant and difficult to transplant; plants may go dormant in midsummer drought but return vigorously each spring and serve as an important nitrogen-fixer and nectar source for native prairie bees.
sources: Flora of the Great Plains, Great Plains Flora Association
